behavior_summary: When a player/actor inspects ('looks' at) a key item, the script reads the key's quality/code and causes the actor to emit a short, context-sensitive description (a bark) describing the key type.

Many specific numeric quality codes are mapped to distinct human-readable key names (examples: a blank key, silver mine key, 'Key of the Conventicler', 'Devon's key', etc.). If no specific match is found a generic 'key' description is used.

Using the key (KEY::use) does not perform unlock logic itself; instead it prepares processing state and spawns the KEYRING::0119 script with the actor id and the item to handle key-use behavior (likely UI or unlocking logic).

The script temporarily sets script-processing/info flags (process-exclude, set info) and uses implies/suspend-style control around bark/spawn operations to avoid reentrancy during these short interactions.
notable_control_flow: Event-driven: main entry is a 'U8P_KEY::look' event that triggers immediate short output (bark).
Branching on Item::getQuality return value: a sequence of comparisons selects the description string to bark.
Around bark and spawn calls the script sets processing/info flags and uses implies/suspend-style control to serialize/guard the operation.
KEY::use delegates runtime behavior to a spawned KEYRING::0119 process rather than performing unlocking inline.
open_questions: Exact numeric-to-string mapping is known for many values but some quality codes observed in the sequence may map to similar or identical 'Key' strings — are there external data tables that canonicalize all quality values?
The precise info id(s) set by 'set info' and their engine-level meaning are not recorded here; what persistent state (if any) is intended by those flags for key items?
Which in-game interactions (e.g., inventory context menu vs. world use) invoke KEY::use versus other key-related scripts (KEYRING::0119) remains inferred from spawn usage.

main_function.name: U8P_KEY::look
main_function.purpose: Provide a concise, context-sensitive textual description (bark) when an actor inspects a key item by reading the item's quality/code and selecting an appropriate short string.
entry_conditions: Event 0 / 'U8P_KEY::look' action: invoked when the player/actor examines or looks at the key item.

function_map.name: U8P_KEY::look
function_map.role: Responds to the 'U8P_KEY::look' event for a key item; inspects item quality and causes the actor to bark an appropriate description.

function_map.name: U8P_KEY::use
function_map.role: Handles the 'use' action for a key by preparing processing state and spawning KEYRING::0119 with the actor and the item; delegates actual use/unlock behavior.

function_map.name: Item::getQuality
function_map.role: Returns an integer quality/code used to distinguish key subtypes; used to select which description to bark.

function_map.name: Item::bark
function_map.role: Emit/display a short text string from the actor/item to the player (used for the key description strings).

function_map.name: spawn (KEYRING::0119)
function_map.role: Start the KEYRING::0119 process with the actor id and item pointer to handle key-use UI or unlocking logic.

external_calls.purpose: Read the numeric quality/code of the key item so the script can branch to the appropriate human-readable description.
Make the actor speak the selected short description string for the key (the visible/played result of 'U8P_KEY::look').
Delegate key-use behavior (likely keyring UI or unlocking logic) to a separate process, passing actor id and item pointer.
Prepare and free the descriptive string passed to Item::bark; bookkeeping around string parameters for bark.

writes.name: script processing/info flags (set info / process-exclude)
writes.likely_effect: Temporarily exclude the object/process from concurrent processing and mark an informational state while producing barks or spawning; controls reentrancy and short-lived script behavior.

quick_facts.behavior_summary_one_liner: When a player/actor inspects ('looks' at) a key item, the script reads the key's quality/code and causes the actor to emit a short, context-sensitive description (a bark) describing the key type.
